coredata.dc:title: Hydrolysis of NH3BH3 and NaBH4 by graphene quantum dots-transition metal nanoparticles for highly effective hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Jialu
Chen, Weifeng
Lv, Guo
Yang, Zihan
Yan, Jiaying
Liu, Xiang
Dai, Zhongxu
coredata.dc:description: Recently, effective hydrogen (H2) evolution upon hydrolysis of different hydrogen storage materials has received much attention. Herein, graphene quantum dots-transition metal nanoparticles (GQDs-TMNPs), with high dispersibility and activity, have been successfully applied in the hydrolysis of both NH3BH3 (AB) and NaBH4 for the first time. GQDs-RhNPs, GQDs-RuNPs, and GQDs-PtNPs are very effective in the hydrolysis of AB and the turnover frequencies (TOFs) can achieve to as high as 656, 384, and 281 molH2·molcat −1 min−1, respectively. Moreover, the synergistic effect between GQDs and TMNPs is explored, and the mechanisms of catalytic hydrolysis of AB and NaBH4 by GQDs-TMNPs are proposed. This work not only paves the way for the development of GQDs-TMNPs nanocatalysts for the different hydrogen storage materials, but also further advances the understanding of the synergistic effects between GQDs and TMNPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogen evolution
Transition metal nanoparticles
Ammonia-borane
Sodium borohydride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The deionized water was prepared in the laboratory.
Catalytic evolution of H2 from AB and NaBH4
Catalytic performances of GQDs-TMNPs in the hydrolysis of AB
Catalytic performances of GQDs-TMNPs in the hydrolysis of NaBH4
The reusability and stability of GQDs-TMNPs on the H2 evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM of (a) GQDs-RhNPs, (b) GQDs-PtNPs and (c) GQDs-RuNPs, and the distribution diagrams of (d) GQDs-RhNPs, (e) GQDs-PtNPs and (f) GQDs-RuNPs.
(a) STEM image and (b) elemental analysis (by EDS) of GQDs-RhNPs.

Comparison of the production of H2 catalyzed by (a) 4 mol ‰ GQDs-TMNPs and (b) 8 mol‰ GQDs-TMNPs; (c) The time plots of H2 production vs. various concent of GQDs-PtNPs with 1 mmol AB; (d) The time plots of H2 production vs. various concentration of AB with 2.25 × 10−3 mmol of GQDs-PtNPs; (e) The time plots of H2 production vs. different temperature with 2.25 mol ‰ GQDs-PtNPs; (f) H2 evolution catalyzed by 2.25 mol ‰ GQDs-PtNPs (black) and 2.25 mol ‰ Glucan-PtNPs (red). TOFs = mol H2 released/(mol catalyst vs. total reaction time (min)) for surface atoms (Table S1). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The XPS of GQDs and GQDs- PtNPs (a) C1s XPS spectrum and (b) O1s XPS spectrum.

(a) Comparison of the production of H2 by GQDs-TMNPs (8 mol ‰, 0.4 M NaOH); (b) The time plots of H2 production vs. various concent of RhNPs; (c) The time plots of H2 production vs. various concentration of NaBH4 by GQDs-RhNPs; (d) The time plots of H2 production vs. different temperature by GQDs-RhNPs (b,c and d in the 0.1 M NaOH, 8 mol ‰).
(a) Plots of volume of H2 vs. time for the hydrolysis of AB catalyzed by GQDs-RhNP; (b) TEM image after the 5th by QDs-RhNPs and the distribution diagram (inset).
The synthesis of GQDs and their stablized TMNPs.
Proposed mechanism for the hydrolysis of AB by GQDs-TMNPs.
Proposed mechanism for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 by GQDs-TMNPs.

head.ce:title: Hydrolysis of NH3BH3 and NaBH4 by graphene quantum dots-transition metal nanoparticles for highly effective hydrogen evolution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Inorganic Nonmetallic Crystalline and Energy Conversion Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, effective hydrogen (H2) evolution upon hydrolysis of different hydrogen storage materials has received much attention. Herein, graphene quantum dots-transition metal nanoparticles (GQDs-TMNPs), with high dispersibility and activity, have been successfully applied in the hydrolysis of both NH3BH3 (AB) and NaBH4 for the first time. GQDs-RhNPs, GQDs-RuNPs, and GQDs-PtNPs are very effective in the hydrolysis of AB and the turnover frequencies (TOFs) can achieve to as high as 656, 384, and 281 molH2·molcat −1 min−1, respectively. Moreover, the synergistic effect between GQDs and TMNPs is explored, and the mechanisms of catalytic hydrolysis of AB and NaBH4 by GQDs-TMNPs are proposed. This work not only paves the way for the development of GQDs-TMNPs nanocatalysts for the different hydrogen storage materials, but also further advances the understanding of the synergistic effects between GQDs and TMNPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 21st century, great efforts have been paid to the development of clean energy, due to the heavy consumption of fossil fuels and serious environmental pollution [1–3]. As an energy medium with the advantages of the environmental friendliness, high calorific value and regeneration capacity, hydrogen (H2) has brought about widespread attention. However, the applications of hydrogen energy have been limited by the issue of hydrogen storage [4–17]. The traditional storage methods, such as compressing or liquefying hydrogen not only need noble equipment but also possess the safety risks. Accordingly, it is very urgent to develop the new types of hydrogen storage. Recently, facile H2 evolution on-site upon hydrolysis of the hydrogen-rich inorganic hydrides, such as NH3BH3 (AB), NaBH4 and B2(OH)4, is considered as the convenient, inexpensive, and effective solution to the energy and environmental problems. Among them, AB is a compound with high hydrogen content (19.6 wt%), and is non-toxic, excellent soluble, and highly stable under ambient conditions. Therefore, AB is considered to be one of the most suitable contenders in chemical hydrogen storage for various applications [2,18–24]. A large number of catalysts have been developed for the hydrogen evolution upon AB hydrolysis. Typically, noble metals such as Pt, Pd, and Ru possess good d-band electronic properties and exhibit the excellent catalytic activity. Their turnover frequencies (TOFs) for hydrogen evolution are commonly higher than those of non-noble metals [25]. However, there are many issues to be addressed for noble metal catalysts in the application, such as high cost, easy to aggregate, and difficulty in recovery. It has been demonstrated that loading metal nanoparticles onto specific carriers or making them to be hybrid materials can facilitate resolving the problems [26,27]. At present, excellent carriers for loading noble metal nanoparticles such as graphene [28,29], mesoporous silica [30], metal-organic frameworks (MOF) [31–33], and nitrogen-doped carbon materials (NCs) [34–43] have been reported.

As a novel carbon material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received much attention because of their outstanding performances and potential applications [44–47]. The band gap energy of GQDs can be regulated in the range of 0–6 eV via controlling their sizes or surface chemical properties, attributed to the quantum confinement effect and the edge effect [48–51]. Compared to traditional semiconductors quantum dots [52], GQDs exhibit excellent hydrophilicity [53], low biotoxicity [54], and stable photoluminescent (PL) properties [55,56]. In fact, our lab has been researching in the synthesis of GQDs, GQDs-derived materials for their applications [57,58]. For example, in 2020, our group first reported graphene quantum dot-stabilized TMNPs catalyzed hydrolysis of tetrahydroxydiboron for hydrogen evolution, in which both atoms of H2 evolution are provided by H2O. Among them, the GQDs have been characterized by UV–vis. Spectrum, FTIR spectra, Raman spectrum, PL spectra, TEM, HRTEM and AFM, and these TMNPs (PtNP, RhNP, RuNP, AuNP, PdNP, IrNP, AgNP, CuNP, FeNP, CoNP and NiNP) have been characterized by TEM, HRTEM and XPS [59]. Herein, we have expanded the application of these TMNPs in the H2 evolution on AB or NaBH4 hydrolysis for the first time. The results demonstrated that GQDs-PtNPs, GQDs-RhNPs and GQDs-RuNPs are efficient in the hydrolysis of AB and NaBH4, while other GQDs-TMNPs don't exhibit the catalytic activities. Especially, GQDs-RhNPs, GQDs-RuNPs and GQDs-PtNPs are highly effective in the hydrolysis of AB and the turnover frequencies (TOFs) can achieve to 656, 384, and 281 molH2·molcat −1 min−1. The dehydrogenation kinetics are studied and the activation energy ( Ea ) are calculated to be 50.06 kJ/mol and 43.94 kJ/mol (based on the Arrhenius equation) for GQDs-PtNPs in the hydrolysis of AB and for GQDs-RhNPs in the hydrolysis of NaBH4, respectively. The present work has proved the versatile GQDs-TMNPs nanocatalysts for highly effective H2 evolution upon the different hydrogen storage candidates, greatly facilitating their applications in the catalytic domain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The deionized water was prepared in the laboratory.
ce:section.ce:para: AgNO3, RhCl3, PtCl4, HAuCl4.3H2O, RuCl3. xH2O, NiCl2.6H2O, FeCl3, CoCl2, CuSO4.5H2O, NaBH4, BH3NH3, IrCl3, K2PdCl4 and glucan, were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The GQDs and GQDs-TMNPs (TMNPs = PtNPs, RhNPs, RuNPs, AuNPs, PdNPs, IrNPs, AgNPs, CuNPs, FeNPs, CoNPs and NiNPs) were synthesized according to our reported method (Scheme 1 ) [59]. Briefly, the GQDs were synthesized from glucan and H2O via a hydrothermal process. Afterwards, the GQDs-TMNPs were prepared by the solution impregnation reduction at 0 °C. Typically, the metallic salt solution was slowly dropped into GQDs aqueous solution and rapidly stirred for 10 min, the NH3BH3 aqueous solution was slowly dropped in the system, which was then sealed. The reaction lasted for 2 h to make the metal ions fully reduced, and the GQDs-TMNPs were obtained finally. Among them, GQDs-RhNPs, GQDs-PtNPs, GQDs-RuNPs are efficient for the hydrolysis of NH3BH3 and NaBH4, while other GQDs-TMNPs don't exhibit the catalytic activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic evolution of H2 from AB and NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the evolution of H2 upon reaction between AB and H2O was conducted at 30 °C. In a 50 mL round bottom flask, a solution of RhNPs (2 × 10−3 mmol, 9 mL) was added with continuous stirring. Next, 0.5 mmol of AB was dissolved in 1 mL water, which was added into the flask. H2 evolution immediately began, and the amount of gas was determined periodically by measuring the displacement of water in burette. The same operation is applied for other GQDs-TMNPs for the hydrolysis of NaBH4.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a TECNAI F-20 electron microscope (JOEL 2100F, Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed in a Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi (ThermoFischer, USA) system spectrometer in an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) chamber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performances of GQDs-TMNPs in the hydrolysis of AB
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic hydrolysis of AB by various GQDs-TMNPs (4 mol ‰) was carried out at 30 °C. It indictates that RhNPs, PtNPs and RuNPs are very efficient for H2 evolution. The values of TOFs are calculated to be as high as 642, 316 and 268 molH2·molCat −1·min−1, respectively (Fig. 3 a), and the order of catalytic activity is as follows: GQDs-RhNPs > GQDs-RuNPs > GQDs-PtNPs (calculation method referring to the literature [59]). When the 8 mol ‰ of GQDs-TMNPs was used (Fig. 3b), the same order has been observed except that the values of TOFs have been changed. The TOFs of GQDs-RuNPs and GQDs-PtNPs are increased to be 384 and 281 molH2·molcat −1 min−1, while the TOF of GQDs-RhNPs is increased to 656 molH2·molCat −1·min−1. It is noteworthy that the TOF of GQDs-PtNPs is even higher than that of Pt@ZIF-8 [33]. Therefore, The effectiveness of the GQDs-PtNPs nanocatalyst was further explored by changing the concents of GQDs-PtNPs and AB. The influence of various amounts of the nanocatalyst (from 2 to 4 mol ‰) have been evaluated at 30 °C for hydrolysis of AB, as shown in Fig. 3c. The rate of hydrolysis will increase with improving the content of GQDs-PtNPs. The slope of the logarithmic plot of H2 evolution vs. concentration of GQDs-PtNPs is 0.82, suggesting it is a first-order reaction in the catalyst concentration. Fig. 3d shows the time plots of H2 production vs. various amouts of AB in the presence of 2.25 mol ‰ GQDs-PtNPs, and the rate of hydrolysis doesn't change obviously with modifying the contents of AB. The slope of logarithmic plot of H2 evolution rate vs. concentration of AB is 0.217, indicating that hydrolysis of AB by GQDs-PtNPs is of zero order in the AB concentration. In order to explore the activation energy (Ea) of the reaction, the hydrolysis of AB at different temperature from 303 to 323 K has also been investigated (Fig. 3e). The results exhibit that the temperature plays a significant influence on the AB hydrolysis, and the reaction rate is obviously increased with improving the temperature. The Ea is calculated to be 50.06 kJ/mol for hydrolysis of AB by GQDs-PtNPs based on Arrhenius equation. As a comparison experiment, the glucan-stabilized PtNPs (Glucan-PtNPs) have also been synthesized and tested under the same condition, as shown in Fig. 3f. It is clear that GQDs-PtNPs exhibit the superior catalytic activity over Glucan-PtNPs for the H2 evolution. This is attributed to the significant synergy between GQDs and PtNPs.

In order to reveal the important synergistic effects between GQDs and PtNPs, the XPS of GQDs and GQDs-PtNPs were measured, as shown in Fig. 4. The XPS of the C 1s of GQDs and GQDs-PtNPs shows the peak of 284.6 eV shifts toward the higher binding energy (284.83 eV) after the GQDs-PtNPs are formed. This variation of binding energy indicates an interaction between GQDs and PtNPs (Fig. 4 a). The spectrum of O 1s of GQDs (Fig. 4b) can be divided into three peaks at 532.1 (C–O), 533.2 (C]O), and 533.8 eV (O–C]O), respectively [46,55]. However, the binding energy of C–O of GQDs-PtNPs was changed from 532.1 eV to 532.7 eV. It is because that the electrons are transferred from GQDs to PtNPs via C–O–Pt, and the efficient electronic coupling results in a negatively charged Pt atoms [59,60]. Therefore, It is conductive to the adsorption of hydrogen protons on the surface of the nanocatalysts, thus accelerating the formation and separation of the hydrogen molecules. From the above, the hydrolysis mechanism of AB by GQDs-PtNPs was proposed (Scheme 2 ) [33]. Briefly, the hydrogen bonds between H2O and NH3BH3 are formed before the reaction, and the rate-determining step is the activation of O–H bond in water molecules and B–H bond in AB. The B–H bonds in AB and O–H bonds in H2O are transferred to the GQDs-PtNPs surface to form the dihydride by the oxidative additions, which not only reduce the active energy of AB and H2O, but also promote the adsorption of hydrogen protons on the suface of the nanocatalyst via the efficient electronic coupling of C–O–Pt. Thus, hydrogen is produced by the reductive elimination with the by-product of NH4 + and B(OH)4 -.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performances of GQDs-TMNPs in the hydrolysis of NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prove that the GQDs-TMNPs are versatile and effective for various hydrogen storage materials, the catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 by different GQDs-TMNPs was carried out at 30 °C. Similar to the AB hydrolysis, only GQDs-RhNPs, GQDs-RuNNPs and GQDs-PtNPs are efficient for H2 evolution, and the TOFs is 217, 190 and 53 molH2·molCat −1·min−1, respectively (Fig. 5 a). Considering the excellent catalytical activity in hydrolysis of both AB and NaNH4, GQDs-RhNPs were chosen to be the typical nanocatalyst for the further research. As is well known, NaBH4 is more stability in alkaline solution than in aqueous solution [61–64]. In order to study the catalytic activity of GQDs-RhNPs for hydrolysis of NaBH4 in alkaline solution, the effects of NaOH on the H2 evolution have been analyzed at various concentrations of NaOH (form 0–0.4 M, Fig. S2). It indicates that the catalytic activity of GQDs-RhNPs for hydrogen evolution upon NaBH4 is more superior at 0.1 M than at other concentrations, with TOFs being 420 molH2·molCat −1·min−1. Therefore, for the exploration of the influence of various amounts of catalyst, the hydrolysis of NaBH4 by GQDs-RhNPs from 2 to 8 mol ‰ have been evaluated at 0.1 M NaOH, as shown in Fig. 5b. The slope of the logarithmic plot of H2 evolution vs. concentration of GQDs-RhNPs is 1.49, suggesting that the reaction order is 1.49 in the catalyst concentration. Fig. 5c shows the time plots of H2 production vs. various amounts of NaBH4 in the presence of 0.004 mol GQDs-RhNPs. The slope of logarithmic plot of H2 evolution rate vs. concentration NaBH4 is −0.79, indicating that the reaction order of hydrolysis of NaBH4 is −0.79 in the NaBH4 concentration. The efficiency of NaBH4 hydrolysis at different temperature from 303 to 323 K With 8 mol ‰ RhNPs has also been investigated in Fig. 5d. It demonstrates that the hydrolysis rate will increase obviously with the temperature improving. The results exhibit that the activation energy (Ea) is 43.94 kJ/mol for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 by the GQDs-RhNPs nanocatalyst.

Besides, the similar mechanism of hydrolysis of NaBH4 to release H2 was also proposed (Scheme 3 ) [61]. In the process, the electronic density of O–H in H2O was decreased, due to the combining of H2O with NaBH4 by hydrogen bonding. The B–H bonds in NaBH4 and O–H bonds in H2O were transferred to the GQDs-RhNPs surface to form the RhNPs-dihydride by the oxidative additions. The GQDs-RhNPs provided both B–H and O–H active sites, which not only reduced the active energy of NaBH4 and H2O, but also promoted the adsorption of hydrogen protons on the suface of the nanocatalyst, thus accelerating the formation of H2 through the reductive elimination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reusability and stability of GQDs-TMNPs on the H2 evolution
ce:section.ce:para: The reusability and stability of catalysts are important for the future practical applications. As the distinguished representative, the GQDs-RhNPs nanocatalyst was used for the test of reusability and stability in AB, because it exhibits the most high catalytic activity among these nanocatalysts. Going through five cycles in 7 min, the AB hydrolysis still keeps the stable catalytic performance, as shown in Fig. 6 a. After the 5th catalyst recycling, the sizes of GQDs-RhNPs almost remain the same as those of the fresh, confirming the GQDs-RhNPs have a good stability with the recycling (Fig. 6b).

We also compared the catalytic performance of our catalyst GQDs-RhNP with recently published literature examples [65–74]. The TOF, Ea and recyclability are listed in Table S2, The results exhibits our present GQDs-RhNPs catalyst are comparable with those obtained with other systems. The TOF is the good. It has been successfully reused 5 times with retaining 95% initial activity.

In summary, GQDs stabilized TMNPs with high dispersibility and activity have been successfully applied in the hydrolysis of both AB and NaBH4 for the first time. We found that only RhNPs, PtNNPs and RuNPs are very efficient for both H2 evolution upon hydrolysis of AB and NaBH4, while other TMNPs (AuNP, PdNP, IrNP, AgNP, CuNP, FeNP, CoNP and NiNP) don't work. Then the reaction kinetics of AB and NaBH4 hydrolysis have been studied and activation energy (Ea) are 50.06 kJ/mol and 43.94 kJ/mol for PhNP in the hydrolysis of AB and for RhNP in the hydrolysis of NaBH4 based on the Arrhenius equation, respectively. RhNP catalyzed hydrogen evolution upon hydrolysis of NaBH4 is more notable at 0.1 M NaOH than other concentrations. In addition, GQDs stabilized TMNPs have been successfully recycled 5 times without significant decrease in catalytic activity. This work has not only proved the versatile GQDs-TMNPs nanocatalysts for the highly effective hydrolysis of different hydrogen storage candidates, but also advances the understanding of the synergistic effects between GQDs and TMNPs, dramatically facilitating their future applications in the catalytic domain.

Despite the achievements from exploring GQDs-stabilized TMNPs in catalytic hydrogen generated from AB and NaBH4, more effort is required to develop non-noble metal catalysts (such as Fe, Co, Ni) for hydrolysis of hydrogen storage materials and other kinds of catalytic reactions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805166), the 111 Project of China (No. D20015), Foundation of Science and Technology Bureau of Yichang City (No. A18-302-a07), the Engineering Research Center of Eco-environment in Three Gorges Reservoir Region, Ministry of Education, China Three Gorges University (No. KF2019-05), China Three Gorges University is gratefully acknowledged.
